sections.title: Overview
sections.content: An ectopic pregnancy occurs when a fertilised egg implants outside the uterine cavity, most commonly in the fallopian tube. It is non-viable and may become life-threatening due to tubal rupture and internal haemorrhage. Suspected ruptured ectopic pregnancy is a medical emergency.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Abdominal or pelvic pain
- Vaginal bleeding
- Amenorrhoea or recent missed period
- Shoulder-tip pain or syncope in internal bleeding
- Hypotension, tachycardia, pallor, shock, or pain out of proportion to visible blood loss

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Consider ectopic pregnancy in any patient of reproductive age with abdominal pain, syncope, shoulder-tip pain, or vaginal bleeding
- Symptoms often occur around 6-8 weeks after the last menstrual period but may present earlier or later
- Risk factors include prior ectopic pregnancy, PID, tubal surgery, IVF, endometriosis, and an IUD in situ
- If suspicion is significant, the patient should not self-transport unless refusing care
- Shock may be disproportionate to visible vaginal blood loss because bleeding is often internal

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Support and reassure the patient while keeping movement to a minimum if significant bleeding or shock is suspected
- Perform a full vital sign survey and repeat it frequently if pain, bleeding, or collapse is evolving
- Administer oxygen if hypoxaemic, shocked, or in respiratory distress
- Consider pregnancy testing where appropriate and where this will not delay transport
- Liaise early with the on-call doctor or RFDS and pre-alert the receiving hospital where relevant

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain ECG if shock, syncope, or diagnostic uncertainty is present
- Gain IV access, including two large-bore cannulas where possible if shock is suspected
- Fluid resuscitation should focus on preserving perfusion while expediting transport and definitive care
- Consider analgesia and antiemetic therapy in line with local protocol and without delaying transfer
- Keep the patient warm if shocked and escalate rapidly for any deterioration

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Evidence for TXA in ruptured ectopic pregnancy is limited and it should not be routine prehospital therapy
- Sensitive communication and psychological support are important
